articles.author: Lorian Belanger
articles.description: Iran, the United States and Israel reached a tentative, two-week ceasefire Wednesday in the war that tore across the Middle East and disrupted the global energy market, with U.S. President Donald Trump pulling back from his threats to destroy Iranian “civilization.”
articles.title: Live updates: US, Israel and Iran agree to a 2-week ceasefire

articles.author: Bassem Mroue, Jon Gambrell, Samy Magdy, Sam Metz
articles.description: Iran, the United States and Israel have agreed to a two-week ceasefire. The 11th-hour deal allowed U.S. President Donald Trump to pull back from his threat to unleash a bombing campaign that would destroy Iranian civilization. Hours after the announcement, Iran and Gulf Arab countries reported new attacks Wednesday. It was not clear if the sporadic attacks would be enough to scuttle the deal. U.S. Vice President JD Vance called it “fragile.” Even before the new strikes were reported, much about the deal was unclear as the sides presented vastly different visions of the terms.
articles.title: US, Israel and Iran agree to a 2-week ceasefire but attack are reported

articles.author: Bridget Brown, Lorian Belanger, Brian P. D. Hannon, Bernard McGhee, Luena Rodriguez-Feo Vileira, Drew Callister, Jim Anderson, Nell Clark
articles.description: Iran says it has accepted a two-week ceasefire in the war, and will negotiate with the United States in Islamabad beginning Friday. President Donald Trump said Tuesday he’s pulling back on his threats to widen attacks.
articles.title: US and Iran agree to a two-week ceasefire

articles.author: Aamer Madhani, Will Weissert, Josh Boak
articles.description: President Donald Trump over the course of a day went from threatening Iran with “annihilation” to proclaiming that the battered Islamic Republic’s leadership had presented a “workable” plan that led him to agree to a two-week ceasefire. Trump said he expects this to pave the way to end the nearly six-week-old war with Iran. The dramatic shift in tenor came as intermediaries, led by Pakistan, worked feverishly to head off a further escalation of the conflict. Even China quietly pulled strings to urge Iran to find a path toward a ceasefire. The president is set to meet on Wednesday with NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte, and the emerging plan to reopen the Strait of Hormuz is expected to be at the center of talks.
articles.title: How Trump moved from threats to a ceasefire with Iran

articles.author: Michelle L. Price
articles.description: NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte is expected to meet with President Donald Trump to try to smooth over the president’s anger with the military alliance over the Iran war. Trump had suggested the U.S. may consider leaving the trans-Atlantic alliance after NATO member countries ignored his call to help reopen a vital shipping waterway that Iran effectively shut, sending gas prices soaring. The Republican president had a warm relationship with Rutte. Their meeting Wednesday comes as the U.S. and Iran agreed late Tuesday to a two-week ceasefire that includes the reopening of the waterway, the Strait of Hormuz.
articles.title: Trump meets NATO leader Rutte as he ponders quitting the alliance

articles.author: The Associated Press
articles.description: Iran, the United States and Israel reached a two-week ceasefire in the war that tore across the Middle East and disrupted the global energy market, with U.S.
articles.title: Photos: US, Israel and Iran agree to a 2-week ceasefire

articles.author: Yuri Kageyama, Matt Ott
articles.description: Wall Street surged in premarket trading as oil prices plunged 16% after the U.S. and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire that includes the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Futures for the S&P 500 jumped 2.7% before the bell Wednesday and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 2.6%. Nasdaq futures soared 3.4%. Benchmark U.S. crude sank $18.43 to $94.52 a barrel, a nearly 16% decline. Brent crude, the international standard dropped $15.54 to $93.73 a barrel. major U.S. airline stocks soared on the steep drop in oil prices. Delta and United jump more than 12% in premarket while American rose 10%.
articles.title: Global shares, Wall Street soar and oil price declines as Iran ceasefire announced

articles.author: Jeff Amy
articles.description: Republican Clay Fuller faces Democrat Shawn Harris in a special election to replace Marjorie Taylor Greene in Congress. The winner of the election Tuesday would serve the rest of Greene's term. Harris led the first round on March 10, but Republicans still hold a clear advantage in the district. President Donald Trump endorsed Fuller, and Fuller says he backs Trump on every issue. Harris calls himself a practical “dirt-road Democrat” and says Fuller cannot put local needs first. The winner would need to run again in separate primaries and a general election later this year to keep the seat.
articles.title: Shawn Harris and Clay Fuller compete in Georgia to replace Marjorie Taylor Greene

articles.author: Scott Bauer
articles.description: Democratic-backed candidate Chris Taylor has won election to the Wisconsin Supreme Court. Her victory Tuesday grows the liberal majority on the court as cases affecting congressional redistricting, union rights and other hot button issues await. Taylor defeated Republican-backed Maria Lazar in the fourth straight victory for liberal court candidates dating back to 2020. Liberals are now guaranteed to hold a majority on the court until at least 2030. Spending and attention on this race were way down given that majority control was not at stake. Liberals will now have a 5-2 majority.
articles.title: Democratic-backed Chris Taylor wins Wisconsin Supreme Court race, growing liberal majority

articles.author: Hannah Fingerhut
articles.description: Michigan U.S. Sen. Elissa Slotkin is in Iowa to support Democratic congressional candidates and gather insights on how Democrats could win over President Donald Trump's supporters. Slotkin, a potential 2028 presidential contender, met with Trump voters to discuss divisiveness and community issues. She also asked what it would take for them to support a Democrat. Slotkin, elected to the Senate in 2024, aims to connect with voters outside her state. She headlined events and held a health care town hall with Iowa state Sen. Sarah Trone Garriott. Slotkin emphasized bipartisan disappointment over health care costs and quality.
articles.title: Sen. Elissa Slotkin meets with Trump voters in Iowa
